text: The surgeon identifies adhesions involving the sigmoid colon and the infundibulopelvic ligament.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and begins opening the pararectal space, progressing towards the Danone-Wheeler's fascia.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and proceeds to open the peritoneum by lateralizing the ureter, extending the dissection towards the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon separates the rectum from the posterior margins of the vagina as part of the initial preparation for a radical hysterectomy.
The surgeon pushes the rectum away after separating it from the posterior margins of the vagina, creating a clear surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection of the vaginal aspect, removing fat tissue up to the level of the external sphincter, with visible separation from the rectum.
The surgeon pushes the bladder down significantly to achieve a clear free margin from the vaginal vault, preparing for a radical parametrectomy.
The surgeon begins bladder dissection by moving laterally due to dense adhesions between the bladder and the vagina.
The surgeon attempts to separate the bladder from the anterior vaginal wall, navigating the challenging dissection plane.
The surgeon places traction on the opened vaginal vault and begins dissection of the bladder, separating it from the anterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon separates the vagina from the bladder, advancing the dissection towards the level of the trigone, with visible tissue planes clearly delineated.
The surgeon opens the Yakubish space, creating a deep dissection that visibly pushes the ureter away from the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the parametrium, focusing on carefully separating the surrounding tissues as part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins marking the parametrium during the dissection, focusing on delineating the tissue boundaries in the surgical field.
Dense fibrosis is observed at the level of the external iliac vein, which is being released using a laparoscopic shearer.

The surgeon has released the fibrosis, revealing the obliterated umbilical artery with the uterine artery originating from it. This marks the beginning of the vascular parametrium, which is the next focus of the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the parametrium, ensuring free margins are achieved for a complete removal of the tissue.
The surgeon releases structures on both sides to ensure complete dissection, then opens the ureteric tunnel to facilitate further dissection due to difficulty in releasing structures on one side.
The surgeon uses scissors, a cold knife technique, to carefully dissect within the plane, navigating around the dense fibrosis previously noted near the external iliac vein.
The surgeon performs extensive lateral dissection to free the ureter, applying traction to mobilize it away from the dissection plane before using coagulation current.
The surgeon completes the dissection up to the entry point of the ureteric tunnel, visibly reducing the probability of fistula formation in this area.
The surgeon uproots the parametrium and flips it over to the medial aspect, tracing the obliterated umbilical artery upwards. Fibrosis from a previous surgery is being released from this area.
The surgeon opens the paravesical space, carefully freeing up fibrotic tissue while being cautious of the obturator nerve located deeper within the surgical plane.
The surgeon performs gentle dissection at the current plane, carefully manipulating the tissue to avoid damage to underlying structures.
The surgeon frees the parametrium from surrounding tissues, preparing it for removal and histopathological examination as part of a radical parametrectomy.
The surgeon lifts the parametrium by coagulating surrounding tissues, leaving the uterine artery intact. The uterine artery is visible and will be coagulated in the subsequent steps.

The surgeon carefully coagulates above the branch of the uterine artery that runs into the ureter, preserving its vascularity. As the uterine artery is coagulated, the parametrium is visibly freed, revealing the deep uterine vein as a critical landmark to be cautious of.
The surgeon identifies the deep uterine vein, a crucial step for preserving the surrounding nerve plexus during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the deep uterine vein embedded within the surrounding fat tissue, which is more prominent due to the patient's obesity, causing obscured visibility.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to dissect the ureteric tunnel, carefully lateralizing the ureter and freeing up tissue margins to minimize the risk of thermal injury.

The surgeon frees the ureter from the uretic tunnel, revealing the uterine vein, which is then coagulated. Following this, dissection progresses into the anterior and cranial planes to free the parametrium, consisting of fibro-fatty tissue, with minimal venous structures that are easily coagulated.

The surgeon mobilizes the ureter laterally, ensuring a free margin at the vault for parametrectomy. A venous structure in the parametrium is coagulated, while further mobilization of the bladder is performed. The ureteric tunnel is freed, and the ureter is pushed down away from the vault.
The surgeon coagulates a venous structure located above the ureter, demonstrating the significant distance created between the ureter and the vagina. The line of coagulation is clearly visible.
The surgeon continues dissection at the level of the vaginal vault, visibly lateralizing the ureter far away from the surgical field to ensure safe margins for the vaginal vault.
The surgeon performs dissection to mobilize the ureter completely free from the lateral wall of the vagina, with the ureter visibly entering the urinary bladder.
